meta.title: Stop the Vallejo Street Takeover
meta.description: SFPD is converting the lower half of Vallejo Street into a police-only tow-away zone. Make your opinion clear before the public meeting on June 26.
meta.ogDescription: SFPD wants to convert the lower half of Vallejo Street into a police-only tow-away zone. Speak up before June 26.
meta.twDescription: SFPD wants to convert the lower half of Vallejo Street into a police-only tow-away zone. Speak up before June 26.

ticker.text: PUBLIC MEETING — JUNE 26 @ 10AM   ◆   CALL-IN: 415-523-2709   ◆   MAKE YOUR OPINION CLEAR   ◆   OUR STREETS. OUR CITY. OUR VOICE.   ◆   PUBLIC MEETING — JUNE 26 @ 10AM   ◆   CALL-IN: 415-523-2709   ◆   MAKE YOUR OPINION CLEAR   ◆   OUR STREETS. OUR CITY. OUR VOICE.   ◆

hero.sub: SFPD is converting the lower half of Vallejo Street into a police-only tow-away zone — removing public access from a street that belongs to all of us.
hero.ctaSecondary: Attend the SFMTA Online Hearing on June 26
hero.shieldAlt: SFPD is converting the lower half of Vallejo Street to a police-only tow-away zone
hero.streetAlt: Police vehicles lining the lower half of Vallejo Street
hero.caption: Lower Vallejo Street — already lined end-to-end with SFPD vehicles

alert.text: This change removes public access to a portion of the street and reserves it exclusively for police vehicles.

plan.h2: A public street, handed over to police parking
plan.card1Title: The lower half of Vallejo St.
plan.card1Body: SFPD is converting the lower portion of the street directly into department use — not a temporary closure.
plan.card2Body: Parking that the neighborhood relies on disappears and is reserved exclusively for police vehicles.
plan.card3Body: Any non-police vehicle is subject to towing — reducing access to residents, visitors, and local businesses.

voices.photoAlt: Supervisor Danny Sauter with SFPD officers in Chinatown
voices.quote: “I know this loss is still not welcome or easy, but I hope it's an okay compromise.”
voices.support: A compromise the neighborhood was never asked about. Public street space shouldn't be given away quietly — the community deserves a say.

questions.q1: Why is public street space being converted to police-only use?
questions.a1: MONEY. There are 2 parking garages adjacent to the police department where they already park many of their cars. Rather than spending money out of their budget to rent more spaces, they want to take away residental parking spots so you can pay for it with your time and money, as you circle around looking even longer for parking, or break down and pay $375/month for a garage space.
questions.q2: How many parking spaces and public access areas will be lost?
questions.a2: The first 60 feet on both sides of Vallejo will be taken up. As driveways then come, the space between the driveway and the end of the new tow-away-zone will likely be too small for a resident to park and we all know the police cars will be hanging out over the end. At a minimum, we're looking at 5-6 spots, but the likelihood is more like 8-10 actual parking spaces will be lost.
questions.a3: None. The residental parking request to rescind was made without considering alternatives. They posted an English-only sign with small print that does not clearly indicate the changes that will be implemented more when they will be implemented.
questions.a4: We are already renowned across the city for our parking scarcity, and this will only make it worse. We circle endlessly looking for a spot, or break down and pay $375 a month for a garage space. We get aggravated seeing someone just sitting in their car—imagine how much worse it will be to see police cars parked and idle, doing nothing. Half the time there are empty spaces in front of the police department; now that will be happening on Vallejo Street too. So as you circle around and around, you'll get to watch those empty spaces taunt you.

action.h2: Learn more. Get involved. Make your voice heard.
action.tagline1: Complete the form below and/or attend the online public hearing on June 26.
action.tagline2: — Our streets. Our city. Our voice. —

pledge.sub1: Put your concerns on the record and demand a public process before any street is handed over.
pledge.sub2: Your comments will be shared with the district supervisor, Danny Sauter, and at the public hearing.

form.nameHint: Your first initial and last name is adequate
form.addressHint: If you don't want your full address listed, a street name or cross street is adequate.
form.emailHint: Your email will not be published or sold.
form.joinMeeting: I will join the public hearing
form.readStatement: Please read my statement for me

success.title: Your comment is on record.
success.body: Thank you — now spread the word and show up June 26.
